title: Why Your Biggest Agency Client Might Not Be Your Most Profitable One
linkedin:
Your largest agency client and your most profitable agency client are rarely the same account.

Most creative and marketing agency owners know this intuitively. Few have actually run the numbers to prove it — and EOFY is the one time of year when twelve months of project and retainer data make it possible to do so clearly.

The pattern is consistent across agencies we work with. Retainer clients — even the smaller ones — generate the margins that keep the lights on. Project clients, particularly the high-volume ones, generate revenue and often consume it at the same time through scope creep, revision rounds absorbed as goodwill, and the commercial concessions that accumulate when a brief arrives with a compressed timeline and the studio is already full.

June makes this worse before EOFY makes it visible. The annual client budget flush drives a surge of project work in the final weeks of the financial year. Briefs arrive fast, timelines are tight, and the negotiating position on scope and pricing is weaker than it would be in a quieter month. The studio looks its busiest. The margin may not reflect it.

The specific number that tracks this is realisation rate: the share of studio time actually billed at the target rate. Most agencies have a sense of it. Few track it by client and by project type, which is the only way to see where it is being given away and to whom.

With EOFY accounts becoming available, now is the right moment to build this picture — before the new year's project commitments are made and before another twelve months of patterns accumulate without being examined.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
Here's something most agency owners suspect but don't get to see clearly until EOFY: the client who generates the most work for the studio is rarely the one generating the most profit.

Retainer clients tend to be the real margin carriers. Project clients, especially in June when everyone's rushing to spend remaining budgets, often generate strong revenue alongside thinner margins — compressed timelines, extra revisions, last-minute specialist costs.

With the financial year wrapping up, it's a good moment to look at which clients actually returned the most per studio hour. The answer usually surprises.

Have you ever mapped your clients by profitability rather than revenue? What did you find?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
For marketing and creative agencies across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria, June is typically the busiest billing month of the year. Annual client budgets expire on 30 June, which drives a surge of project work into the studio in the final weeks of the financial year.

But a busy studio and a profitable one are different things.

The financial insight that EOFY makes available for agency owners is client-level profitability — not revenue, but actual margin after studio time is accounted for. The pattern that typically emerges is that retainer clients carry the business's margin, while high-volume project clients generate revenue that can be thinner than it appears once revision rounds, scope concessions, and compressed-timeline costs are factored in.

The specific measure to track is realisation rate: the share of studio hours that convert to billed revenue at the target rate. Most agency owners have a rough sense of it. Few have mapped it by client and by work type, which is the only way to see where margin is being given away and by how much.

With twelve months of project and retainer data available at EOFY, now is the natural time to build this picture. ProfitPulse works with professional services and creative businesses across the East Coast to develop exactly this kind of client-level financial clarity.

Read the full piece on the blog.
